round-B_SeniorLead_reasoning: The abstract and title clearly describe a Transformer architecture applied to machine translation and English parsing (mentions BLEU scores for English→German/French and constituency parsing), with outputs and evaluation in textual tasks—no mention of audio generation or audio outputs—so it fails the generative-audio focus. Both junior reviewers similarly noted the lack of audio focus. Conclusion (one-sentence): Because the paper’s scope is NLP/MT/parsing with textual outputs and contains no evidence of generative audio models or audio output, it should be excluded at this stage.

The abstract describes a Transformer for modeling and generating music (minute-long compositions, motif continuations, accompaniments) but does not explicitly state that the model outputs waveform/audio (it references datasets JSB Chorales and Piano-e-Competition without clarifying audio vs. symbolic representation); junior reviewers disagreed (one flagged insufficient evidence, one assumed audio). Missing explicit evidence of final audio-domain output, so keep for further review (score 3).

Both junior reviewers noted the abstract/title lack any mention of audio; the paper is a broad comparative review of generative model classes (VAEs, GANs, normalizing flows, energy-based, autoregressive) without explicit indication that the output domain or main focus is audio. Missing evidence: no statement that it introduces or centers on generative audio models, audio applications, or audio-specific evaluations—so cannot confirm inclusion from title/abstract alone. Therefore keep the paper for full review to verify audio relevance. One-sentence explanation: scored as 3 (uncertain) because the title/abstract do not explicitly indicate an audio focus, so more evidence from the main text is needed.

The title and abstract describe a qualitative analysis of NeurIPS 2020 broader impact statements (themes like valence, specificity, uncertainty, and impacts such as bias, environment, labor, privacy) with no mention of generative audio models, audio outputs, or audio-focused applications, so it is out of scope for a generative-audio review (junior reviewers likewise noted the absence of audio focus).

Title and abstract discuss mitigating computing's negative impacts via changes to peer review, with no mention of audio, generative audio models, speech/music, or audio outputs; both junior reviewers similarly noted the absence of audio-focused evidence. Missing evidence needed to include this paper would be explicit treatment of generative audio models or audio applications (model, dataset, audio outputs, or audio-specific use cases/experiments). Therefore this is likely out-of-scope for the generative-audio collection.

This paper directly addresses generative-audio outputs by detecting AI-synthesized (fake) voices (audio-only), reports multi-dataset/multilingual experiments and robustness checks that align with the inclusion criteria for generative audio model applications, and thus should advance to full review (junior reviewers: one recommended inclusion, one was uncertain).

The title and abstract explicitly describe using "deep generative neural networks" for social music composition (audio output) and report a user study on AI’s role in pair-wise music co-creation, indicating a generative-audio application as the primary focus; one junior reviewer flagged missing model-detail specifics but that does not warrant exclusion at this high-recall stage.
題目與摘要明確指出本工作是針對「機器產生的音頻攻擊」做檢測/防禦（利用多通道頻譜、深度學習與無監督領域自適應），並未提出或分析任何生成式音訊模型或以生成為主要研究焦點；兩位初審意見也分別給予 3 與 2（偏向排除），因此本稿較可能不符合以生成式音訊模型為主要主題的收錄標準。簡短說明：因為缺乏提出或以生成模型為核心的證據，故評為「可能排除」。

Title and abstract describe a cross-country public-opinion survey about AI generally and contain no mention of audio, generative audio models, audio outputs, or audio-specific topics (so it fails the generative-audio focus); I note the junior reviewers were split but the absence of any audio-related evidence justifies strong exclusion.

Title and abstract clearly describe a survey of biometric recognition methods (physiological/behavioral traits, unimodal/multimodal) with no mention of audio generation, generative models, or audio outputs — both junior reviewers also flagged it as unrelated; therefore it does not meet the generative-audio focus.

The abstract develops provable copyright protections for generative models and reports experiments on language and image models (transformers and diffusion) with no mention of audio models or audio outputs, so it does not satisfy the requirement that the paper’s primary focus and outputs be generative audio; I reviewed the two junior reviewers’ notes (both flagged the lack of audio evidence) and therefore strongly exclude.

The title and abstract explicitly focus on computer vision and workshops about ethical/social dimensions of creative computer-vision applications (CVPR, ECCV, ICCV), with no mention of audio, generative audio models, or audio outputs—so it does not meet the inclusion criterion that the paper’s primary focus/output be audio; two junior reviewers rated it as uncertain (3), but the explicit modality mismatch supports exclusion.

The title and abstract clearly describe a wav-to-wav unsupervised generative audio model for cross-domain singing voice conversion (meets the generative-audio focus, real-time waveform generator, and evaluation claims), and while one junior reviewer flagged uncertainty about whether this is a full long-form paper, there is no negative evidence or topic mismatch—so I favor inclusion but retain slight caution about publication form. Score: 4 (可能納入).

The abstract describes a broad survey of deep learning for audio that mentions generative models among many topics but does not indicate generative audio is the primary focus or confirm this is a full-length research paper (both junior reviewers gave score 3), so I also rate it 3 (uncertain / needs more evidence).

The abstract outlines an ethical/anticipatory analysis of deepfakes (video and audio) in elections using hypothetical scenarios and interventions but does not introduce or analyze a generative audio model or an audio-only application, so it does not meet the generative-audio-model focus required for inclusion (junior reviewers were uncertain but lacked evidence of a technical/audio-model contribution).

The title and abstract clearly describe generating co-speech gestures (motion) from text, audio, and speaker identity—audio is only an input and the primary output is non-audio—so it does not meet the inclusion criterion of a paper whose primary focus/output is generative audio (I have considered the junior reviews: 3 and 2).
標題與摘要明確描述的是「在音樂條件下生成舞蹈動作（動態動作圖、節拍對齊、動作/風格一致性等）」，主要輸出為舞蹈/運動序列而非音頻生成或音頻輸出，故不符合以生成音頻模型且輸出域完全為音頻的納入標準；兩位初審也指出相同判斷。基於此給予強烈排除。

The title and abstract focus on NSF merit review criteria and the National Science Digital Library—discussing funding, Broader Impacts, and information science—with no mention of audio, generative audio models, audio outputs, or related methods; both junior reviewers similarly flagged lack of audio relevance, so exclude.

The title and abstract analyze NSF’s Broader Impacts and dissemination practices and contain no mention of generative audio models, audio outputs, or related technical/audio topics (both junior reviewers likewise flagged lack of audio relevance), so it does not meet the generative-audio focus criteria.

The title and abstract focus on crowdsourcing for anticipating societal impacts of governmental algorithmic decision-making tools and make no mention of generative audio models, audio outputs (speech, music), or audio-specific applications; junior reviewers gave 3 and 2 reflecting uncertainty vs exclusion, but the absence of any audio-related evidence means the paper likely falls outside the generative-audio scope. (Brief explanation: No mention of generative audio or audio-domain outputs, so likely not eligible.)

The title and abstract clearly discuss impact assessment instruments for political governance and policy integration without any mention of audio, generative models, speech, music, or audio outputs—so it does not meet the generative-audio focus; junior reviewers gave 3 and 2, and I conclude strong exclusion based on explicit non-audio topicality.

The title and abstract focus on algorithmic impact assessments and accountability across domains and contain no mention of audio, generative audio models, or audio outputs, so the paper does not meet the generative-audio inclusion criteria; junior reviewers also noted lack of audio evidence (scores 3 and 2). Score: 1 (強烈排除).
摘要與標題討論一般演算法系統的社會技術性傷害與分類法，未提及音訊或生成式音訊模型／以音訊為主要輸出，因此不符合以生成式音訊為主題的納入標準；兩位初審給出不確定，但缺少任何音訊相關證據支持納入。

Abstract describes a broad survey of malicious uses of AI across digital/physical/political domains and makes high-level recommendations, with no mention of generative audio, speech/music synthesis, audio deepfakes, or audio-only outputs; both junior reviewers also rated it as uncertain (3). Lacking explicit evidence that the paper’s primary focus is generative audio or audio outputs, I cannot include it—recommend 3 (need full text to confirm audio relevance).

Both junior reviewers flagged lack of audio focus; the title and abstract clearly address ethical/social risks of large-scale language models (text/LMs) with no mention of generative audio, speech, music, or audio outputs—so it does not meet the generative-audio primary-focus criterion (missing evidence: any generative audio model/application or audio-output focus). Therefore I lean to exclusion (but not the strongest exclusion).

Junior reviewers observed the abstract is broad and likely focused on image/video deepfakes; the title and abstract discuss creation and detection of "deepfakes" generically and list trends, defenses, and research gaps but contain no mention of audio, speech, or audio-specific models/datasets. Inclusion requires the paper’s primary focus/output domain to be audio (generative audio models or audio-only applications), which is not evidenced here. Because absence of explicit audio mention is not definitive exclusion but leaves uncertainty, this should pass to full review to confirm whether audio is a primary focus. Score explanation (one sentence): 3 — insufficient evidence in title/abstract to confirm an audio-focused paper, so keep for full review to verify audio content.

The abstract describes Armor as a benchmark dataset and meta-evaluation framework for assessing objective evaluations of computer-generated music, providing audio test cases and human judgment scores and thus directly targets evaluation of generative-audio outputs (even though it does not propose a new generative model itself). Junior reviewers were split (4 and 3); no exclusionary evidence appears in the title/abstract, though the abstract does not explicitly state full-paper status or whether the dataset contains outputs from multiple generative models. Given the topic relevance to generative music evaluation and the high-recall stage, I recommend keeping it in flow (可能納入).

The title and abstract describe a symbolic music generative model conditioned on human sentiment (valence/arousal) using a Transformer-GAN with efficient attention and a discriminator, which clearly targets generative music and meets the inclusion criteria for generative audio models (one junior reviewer noted mild uncertainty about symbolic vs raw audio outputs, and the abstract doesn't explicitly state paper length, but there is no exclusionary evidence), so proceed to full review.

The title and abstract describe a concrete full-research contribution (a conditional VAE extension of RAVE using pitch-activation aux data) targeting waveform audio synthesis for polyphonic music and include a perceptual evaluation (MUSHRA on MAESTRO), meeting the generative-audio-model focus with no evidence it is only an extended abstract (junior reviewers split 3 vs. 5; I side with inclusion).

Title and abstract clearly describe a symbolic (MIDI) generative music model that learns discrete latent codes for loop generation, reports fidelity/diversity improvements, and provides code—this matches the inclusion criteria for generative audio models (symbolic audio output) and no exclusion criteria are met; I considered the two junior reviews (one uncertain about the audio-domain interpretation, one strongly include) and conclude inclusion is warranted.

The abstract explicitly focuses on symbolic music generation (MIDI, note/chord/section-level modeling) rather than generating audio waveforms, so it fails the requirement that the output domain be entirely audio and should be excluded at Stage 1.

Both junior reviewers rated this a 3. Title/abstract describe self-supervised and contrastive representation learning for spoken question answering (SQA), focusing on understanding speech and predicting answers, with no mention of a generative audio model or audio synthesis as the primary output; therefore it likely falls outside a generative-audio focus but evidence is only from title/abstract so per stage guidance I remain conservative. Missing: explicit introduction/analysis of a generative audio model or audio-synthesis output. Score: 3.

The abstract presents a generative inpainting model for MIDI piano performances and an Ableton plugin (i.e., symbolic/MIDI output), so it's clearly relevant to generative music but ambiguous with respect to the inclusion criterion that the output domain be entirely audio (waveforms) — junior reviewers were split (one flagged MIDI-only as a concern, the other treated it as in-scope); I recommend passing to full review to confirm whether the paper includes audio-waveform output or audio-domain evaluation and that it is a full long-form paper.

摘要與標題均描述一個以多目標、多階段聯合學習提升語音增強/去噪泛化能力的輕量 DCN 模型，重點在掩碼、映射與傳統估計器參數的融合，並未提及任何典型的生成式音訊方法（如擴散模型、生成採樣或音訊合成應用），因此無法確認屬於「生成式音訊模型」研究；兩位初審者亦給予不確定(3)。缺少的關鍵證據：全文是否採用或討論生成式建模方法、或將其用於生成式音訊應用。基於召回導向的 Stage‑1 篩選，建議保留進入全文審查。

The abstract presents a full research contribution that applies diffusion models to generate symbolic music (sequence generation and infilling in a VAE latent space), which falls squarely under generative music/audio models—one junior reviewer noted ambiguity about symbolic (vs. waveform) outputs but the other supported inclusion, and there is no exclusionary evidence, so I recommend inclusion.

The title and abstract clearly target generative-audio (melody) composition with a human-in-the-loop BO system—aligning with inclusion criteria—while one junior reviewer noted uncertainty about whether this is a full-length paper and the other recommended inclusion; because topic relevance is strong but full-paper status isn’t confirmed, I mark it as likely include.

The title and abstract clearly present a generative music model (RNN-based hierarchical VAE) for multi-track symbolic music (pianoroll) evaluated on the Lakh Pianoroll Dataset with audio samples/code available, which fits the generative-audio (music) inclusion criteria and presents no exclusionary evidence (junior reviewers split 3 vs 5, but absence of negative signals and explicit evaluation justify strong inclusion).

Both junior reviewers rated this as uncertain (3). The abstract describes a probabilistic convolutional beamformer for simultaneous dereverberation and denoising (signal enhancement) and does not present or evaluate a generative audio model, audio synthesis outputs, or generative objectives—so it appears out of scope for a generative-audio-models track because it focuses on enhancement/beamforming rather than generating new audio content; therefore I lean toward exclusion. (Missing evidence: no mention of audio synthesis, generative model architecture, sample generation, or applications involving creating audio.)

The title and abstract explicitly propose a novel generative model (MIDI-Sandwich) for symbolic single-track music (MIDI) and report experiments on the Nottingham dataset—meeting the generative-audio focus and full-paper criteria (one junior reviewer was cautious about waveform vs symbolic output, but the explicit MIDI/Nottingham evidence supports symbolic music generation), so the paper should be included for full review.

The abstract describes a clear generative method for musical inpainting using a VAE latent space and an RNN to generate measures that connect past and future musical contexts, and reports objective and subjective evaluations and interactive use — evidence that it’s a generative-music paper. However, the wording (“musical score inpainting”, “score”) strongly suggests the output is symbolic (scores/MIDI) rather than audio waveforms, and the abstract does not explicitly state that the model produces audio signals, so it is unclear whether the paper meets the requirement that the output domain is entirely audio. Junior reviewers disagreed (one noted the ambiguity, the other assumed audio), which reinforces that clarification is needed. Therefore: Score 3 — ambiguous/needs clarification on whether the final output is audio (waveform) vs. symbolic score before deciding inclusion.

Both junior reviewers gave 3; the abstract describes a broad survey of deep learning for audio that explicitly mentions generative models among many topics but does not indicate generative audio models are the primary focus (nor confirm full-paper form), so more evidence from the full text is needed—score 3.

The abstract describes an open-source web template for interactive demonstrations of symbolic musical machine-learning models (with browser audio rendering) and emphasizes demonstration methodology rather than introducing or analyzing generative audio models or audio-only outputs; JuniorNano rated inclusion (5) likely due to the audio rendering mention, while JuniorMini rated uncertain (3) noting the demo/method focus. Missing evidence: whether this is a full-length research paper and whether the primary contribution is a generative audio model or audio-domain evaluation rather than a tooling/demo contribution. Given the weak signal on core-topic relevance but no explicit exclusionary evidence, I recommend a conservative "3" (uncertain / needs more evidence).

摘要描述了一個用於生成音樂序列的 Convolutional Variational Recurrent NN 並以 Variable Markov Oracle 的 Information Rate 評估其對訓練資料音樂結構的擬合，顯示為生成音樂的研究方向，但未明確指出最終輸出為原始音訊波形或僅為符號（例如 MIDI），且無法從標題/摘要確定是否為完整長文；兩位初審給分分別為 3 與 5，因此在證據不足時保守放行並標為 3（需要更多證據）。
